```{r nn_split_summary}
  Reports = c(nrow(x_train_scaled), nrow(x_test_scaled)),
  Serious_Rate = c(mean(y_train), mean(y_test))
)

split_summary %>%
  mutate(
    Reports = comma(Reports),
    Serious_Rate = percent(Serious_Rate, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Set", "Reports", "Serious outcome rate"),
    caption = "The train and test sets keep similar serious outcome rates. This helps make the evaluation fair."
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

#### How the Three Models Work

Model 1 is the baseline shallow neural network. It has two hidden layers. Its job is to answer a simple question: can a basic neural network learn useful signal from the variables identified in the EDA?

Model 2 is the deep dense neural network. It uses more layers, batch normalization, dropout, and regularization. Its job is to test whether a deeper model can learn more complex relationships, such as age interacting with polypharmacy or drug class interacting with number of reactions.

Model 3 is the wide and deep neural network. It starts with larger hidden layers, which gives it more capacity to learn broader patterns. Strong dropout is used so the larger model does not simply memorize the training data.

Each model outputs a probability between 0 and 1. A value closer to 1 means the model thinks the report is more likely to be serious. The final class is assigned using a 0.50 threshold.

```{r nn_helper_functions, include=FALSE, results = FALSE}
evaluate_binary_model <- function(model, x_test_scaled, y_test, model_name) {
  predicted_prob <- as.numeric(predict(model, x_test_scaled, verbose = 0))
  predicted_class <- ifelse(predicted_prob >= 0.50, 1, 0)

  true_positive <- sum(predicted_class == 1 & y_test == 1)
  true_negative <- sum(predicted_class == 0 & y_test == 0)
  false_positive <- sum(predicted_class == 1 & y_test == 0)
  false_negative <- sum(predicted_class == 0 & y_test == 1)

  accuracy <- mean(predicted_class == y_test)
  precision <- ifelse(true_positive + false_positive == 0, NA_real_,
                      true_positive / (true_positive + false_positive))
  recall <- ifelse(true_positive + false_negative == 0, NA_real_,
                   true_positive / (true_positive + false_negative))
  f1 <- ifelse(is.na(precision) | is.na(recall) | precision + recall == 0, NA_real_,
               2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall))

  roc_object <- pROC::roc(y_test, predicted_prob, quiet = TRUE)
  auc_value <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(roc_object))

  list(
    metrics = data.frame(
      Model = model_name,
      AUC = auc_value,
      Accuracy = accuracy,
      Precision = precision,
      Recall = recall,
      F1 = f1
    ),
    predictions = data.frame(
      Model = model_name,
      Actual = factor(ifelse(y_test == 1, "Serious", "Not serious"),
                      levels = c("Not serious", "Serious")),
      Actual_Numeric = y_test,
      Predicted_Class = factor(ifelse(predicted_class == 1, "Serious", "Not serious"),
                               levels = c("Not serious", "Serious")),
      Predicted_Probability = predicted_prob
    )
  )
}

history_to_data_frame <- function(history, model_name) {
  metric_names <- names(history$metrics)
  n_epochs <- length(history$metrics$loss)

  get_metric <- function(metric_name) {
    if (metric_name %in% metric_names) {
      history$metrics[[metric_name]]
    } else {
      rep(NA_real_, n_epochs)
    }
  }

  data.frame(
    Model = model_name,
    Epoch = seq_len(n_epochs),
    Training_Loss = get_metric("loss"),
    Validation_Loss = get_metric("val_loss"),
    Training_Accuracy = get_metric("accuracy"),
    Validation_Accuracy = get_metric("val_accuracy")
  )
}

compact_test_summary <- function(result_object, history_object) {
  metrics <- result_object$metrics
  predictions <- result_object$predictions
  n_epochs_run <- length(history_object$metrics$loss)

  data.frame(
    `Epochs run` = n_epochs_run,
    `Test AUC` = round(metrics$AUC, 4),
    `Test accuracy` = round(metrics$Accuracy, 4),
    `Test precision` = round(metrics$Precision, 4),
    `Test recall` = round(metrics$Recall, 4),
    `Test F1` = round(metrics$F1, 4),
    `Test reports` = nrow(predictions),
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}

compact_confusion_summary <- function(result_object) {
  result_object$predictions %>%
    count(Actual, Predicted_Class) %>%
    group_by(Actual) %>%
    mutate(`Row percent` = percent(n / sum(n), accuracy = 0.1)) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    rename(`Actual outcome` = Actual,
           `Predicted outcome` = Predicted_Class,
           `Number of reports` = n)
}

make_callbacks <- function() {
  list(
    callback_early_stopping(
      monitor = "val_loss",
      patience = 15,
      restore_best_weights = TRUE
    ),
    callback_reduce_lr_on_plateau(
      monitor = "val_loss",
      patience = 7,
      factor = 0.50
    )
  )
}

epochs_nn <- 100
batch_size_nn <- 512
```

### Model 1: Baseline Shallow Neural Network

The baseline model is intentionally simple. It gives the project a fair starting point before moving to deeper models. If this model performs well, it means the main predictors from the EDA already contain strong signal.

```{r nn_model_1, echo=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
if (file.exists('model_baseline.keras')) {
  model_baseline <- load_model('model_baseline.keras')
  history_baseline <- readRDS('history_baseline.rds')
  message("Model loaded from file")
} else {
    model_baseline <- keras_model_sequential(input_shape = ncol(x_train_scaled)) %>%
  layer_dense(units = 100, activation = "relu", name = "hidden_layer_1") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.30, name = "dropout_1") %>%
  layer_dense(units = 50, activation = "relu", name = "hidden_layer_2") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.20, name = "dropout_2") %>%
  layer_dense(units = 1, activation = "sigmoid", name = "output_layer")

model_baseline %>% compile(
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  loss = "binary_crossentropy",
  metrics = c("accuracy")
)

history_baseline <- model_baseline %>% fit(
  x = x_train_scaled,
  y = y_train,
  epochs = epochs_nn,
  batch_size = batch_size_nn,
  validation_split = 0.20,
  callbacks = make_callbacks(),
  verbose = 0
)

  save_model(model_baseline, 'model_baseline.keras')
  saveRDS(history_baseline, 'history_baseline.rds')
  message("Model and history trained and saved to disk.")
}

result_baseline <- evaluate_binary_model(
  model = model_baseline,
  x_test_scaled = x_test_scaled,
  y_test = y_test,
  model_name = "Baseline shallow neural network"
)
```

The training code is shown above because it is the main modeling step. The model trains for up to 100 epochs, but the epoch by epoch output is hidden with `verbose = 0` so the report stays readable. Early stopping keeps the best validation version of the model.

```{r nn_model_1_test_summary}
compact_test_summary(result_baseline, history_baseline) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Baseline neural network test set results"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

```{r nn_model_1_confusion_summary}
compact_confusion_summary(result_baseline) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Baseline neural network test set classification counts"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

### Model 2: Deep Dense Neural Network

The second model is deeper. It is designed to learn layered patterns in the data. Early layers can learn broad patterns such as whether a report looks high risk overall. Later layers can combine those patterns into more specific signals, such as older age plus many drugs plus a high risk drug class.

```{r nn_model_2, echo=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

if (file.exists('model_deep.keras')) {
  model_deep <- load_model('model_deep.keras')
  history_deep <- readRDS('history_deep.rds')
  message("Model loaded from file.")
} else{
model_deep <- keras_model_sequential(input_shape = ncol(x_train_scaled)) %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 256,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "dense_layer_1"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_1") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.40, name = "dropout_1") %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 128,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "dense_layer_2"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_2") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.40, name = "dropout_2") %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 64,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "dense_layer_3"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_3") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.30, name = "dropout_3") %>%
  layer_dense(units = 32, activation = "relu", name = "dense_layer_4") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.20, name = "dropout_4") %>%
  layer_dense(units = 1, activation = "sigmoid", name = "output_layer")

model_deep %>% compile(
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  loss = "binary_crossentropy",
  metrics = c("accuracy")
)

history_deep <- model_deep %>% fit(
  x = x_train_scaled,
  y = y_train,
  epochs = epochs_nn,
  batch_size = batch_size_nn,
  validation_split = 0.20,
  callbacks = make_callbacks(),
  verbose = 0
)

save_model(model_deep, 'model_deep.keras')
saveRDS(history_deep, 'history_deep.rds')
message("Model and history saved to disk.")
}

result_deep <- evaluate_binary_model(
  model = model_deep,
  x_test_scaled = x_test_scaled,
  y_test = y_test,
  model_name = "Deep dense neural network"
)
```

This model uses more layers than the baseline, so the point is not just to get a prediction. The point is to check whether added depth improves test performance after regularization and early stopping.

```{r nn_model_2_test_summary}
compact_test_summary(result_deep, history_deep) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Deep dense neural network test set results"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

```{r nn_model_2_confusion_summary}
compact_confusion_summary(result_deep) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Deep dense neural network test set classification counts"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

### Model 3: Wide and Deep Neural Network

The third model gives the neural network more capacity at the beginning. This helps it search through many possible combinations of predictors. Dropout and regularization are included to keep the model from becoming too dependent on any one feature.

```{r nn_model_3, echo=TRUE, results='hide', message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}

if (file.exists('model_wide_deep.keras')) {
  model_wide_deep <- load_model('model_wide_deep.keras')
  history_wide_deep <- readRDS('history_wide_deep.rds')
  message('Model loaded from file.')
} else {
model_wide_deep <- keras_model_sequential(input_shape = ncol(x_train_scaled)) %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 512,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "wide_layer_1"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_1") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.50, name = "dropout_1") %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 256,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "wide_layer_2"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_2") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.40, name = "dropout_2") %>%
  layer_dense(
    units = 128,
    activation = "relu",
    kernel_regularizer = regularizer_l2(0.001),
    name = "deep_layer_3"
  ) %>%
  layer_batch_normalization(name = "batch_norm_3") %>%
  layer_dropout(rate = 0.30, name = "dropout_3") %>%
  layer_dense(units = 1, activation = "sigmoid", name = "output_layer")

model_wide_deep %>% compile(
  optimizer = optimizer_adam(learning_rate = 0.001),
  loss = "binary_crossentropy",
  metrics = c("accuracy")
)

history_wide_deep <- model_wide_deep %>% fit(
  x = x_train_scaled,
  y = y_train,
  epochs = epochs_nn,
  batch_size = batch_size_nn,
  validation_split = 0.20,
  callbacks = make_callbacks(),
  verbose = 0
)

save_model(model_wide_deep, 'model_wide_deep.keras')
saveRDS(history_wide_deep, 'history_wide_deep.rds')
message('Model trained and saved to disk.')

}

result_wide_deep <- evaluate_binary_model(
  model = model_wide_deep,
  x_test_scaled = x_test_scaled,
  y_test = y_test,
  model_name = "Wide and deep neural network"
)
```

This model has the most capacity of the three. It is useful only if the larger architecture improves performance on the test set, not just on the training data. That is why the test set metrics are shown directly below.

```{r nn_model_3_test_summary}
compact_test_summary(result_wide_deep, history_wide_deep) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Wide and deep neural network test set results"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

```{r nn_model_3_confusion_summary}
compact_confusion_summary(result_wide_deep) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Wide and deep neural network test set classification counts"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  )
```

### Model Performance

The table below summarizes the test set results. AUC is the most important metric here because it measures how well the model ranks serious reports above reports that are not serious. Accuracy is also shown, but it can be less informative when serious outcomes are common in the dataset.

```{r nn_model_comparison_table}
comparison_results <- bind_rows(
  result_baseline$metrics,
  result_deep$metrics,
  result_wide_deep$metrics
) %>%
  mutate(
    Architecture = c(
      "Input to 100 to 50 to 1",
      "Input to 256 to 128 to 64 to 32 to 1",
      "Input to 512 to 256 to 128 to 1"
    )
  ) %>%
  select(Model, Architecture, AUC, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1)

comparison_results %>%
  mutate(across(c(AUC, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1), ~ round(.x, 4))) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = "Test set performance for the three neural network models"
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
```

```{r nn_model_comparison_plot, fig.height=5.6, fig.width=10}
comparison_long <- comparison_results %>%
  select(Model, AUC, Accuracy, F1) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(AUC, Accuracy, F1),
    names_to = "Metric",
    values_to = "Value"
  )

ggplot(comparison_long, aes(x = reorder(Model, Value), y = Value, fill = Metric)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.75), width = 0.65, alpha = 0.92) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = round(Value, 3)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.75),
    vjust = -0.35,
    size = 3.5,
    color = "grey20"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
    legend.title = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Metric value",
    title = "Model Performance on the Same Test Set",
    subtitle = "AUC evaluates ranking quality, while accuracy and F1 summarize classification at the 0.50 threshold"
  )
```

## ROC Curve Comparison

The ROC curve shows how well each model separates serious reports from reports that are not serious across all possible thresholds. Curves closer to the upper left corner indicate stronger ranking performance.

```{r nn_roc_curves, fig.height=6, fig.width=9.5}
prediction_all <- bind_rows(
  result_baseline$predictions,
  result_deep$predictions,
  result_wide_deep$predictions
)

roc_data <- prediction_all %>%
  group_by(Model) %>%
  group_modify(~ {
    roc_object <- pROC::roc(.x$Actual_Numeric, .x$Predicted_Probability, quiet = TRUE)
    roc_coords <- pROC::coords(
      roc_object,
      x = "all",
      ret = c("specificity", "sensitivity"),
      transpose = FALSE
    )
    data.frame(
      False_Positive_Rate = 1 - roc_coords$specificity,
      True_Positive_Rate = roc_coords$sensitivity
    )
  }) %>%
  ungroup()

auc_labels <- comparison_results %>%
  transmute(Model, Label = paste0(Model, " AUC ", round(AUC, 4)))

ggplot(roc_data, aes(x = False_Positive_Rate, y = True_Positive_Rate, color = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.15) +
  geom_abline(linetype = "dashed", color = "grey60") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2", labels = auc_labels$Label) +
  coord_equal() +
  theme(legend.title = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "False positive rate",
    y = "True positive rate",
    title = "ROC Curves for Serious Outcome Prediction",
    subtitle = "Higher curves mean stronger separation between serious and not serious reports"
  )
```

## Prediction Separation for the Best Model

The next plot uses the model with the highest AUC. It shows whether predicted probabilities are meaningfully different for serious and not serious reports. Ideally, serious reports should be shifted toward higher predicted probabilities.

```{r nn_best_model_setup, include=FALSE}
best_model_name <- comparison_results %>%
  arrange(desc(AUC)) %>%
  slice(1) %>%
  pull(Model)

best_model <- switch(
  best_model_name,
  "Baseline shallow neural network" = model_baseline,
  "Deep dense neural network" = model_deep,
  "Wide and deep neural network" = model_wide_deep
)

best_predictions <- prediction_all %>%
  filter(Model == best_model_name)
```

```{r nn_probability_distribution, fig.height=5.6, fig.width=9.5}
ggplot(best_predictions, aes(x = Predicted_Probability, fill = Actual)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  theme(legend.title = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    x = "Predicted probability of a serious outcome",
    y = "Density",
    title = paste("Prediction Separation for", best_model_name),
    subtitle = "Serious reports should generally receive higher predicted probabilities"
  )
```

## Confusion Matrix for the Best Model

The confusion matrix shows the practical classification result when the predicted probability is converted into a class using a 0.50 threshold.

```{r nn_confusion_matrix_best, fig.height=5.2, fig.width=7}
confusion_df <- best_predictions %>%
  count(Actual, Predicted_Class) %>%
  group_by(Actual) %>%
  mutate(Row_Percent = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup()

ggplot(confusion_df, aes(x = Predicted_Class, y = Actual, fill = Row_Percent)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(comma(n), "\n", percent(Row_Percent, accuracy = 0.1))),
    size = 4.2,
    color = "grey15",
    lineheight = 0.95
  ) +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#c6dbef",   # light blue (low values)
    high = "#de2d26",  # red (high values)
    labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  theme(
    panel.grid = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right"
  ) +
  labs(
    x = "Predicted class",
    y = "Actual class",
    fill = "Row percent",
    title = paste("Confusion Matrix for", best_model_name),
    subtitle = "Each cell shows the number of reports and the row percentage"
  )
``` 

```{r nn_best_model_metrics, results='show'}
cm_best <- confusionMatrix(
  factor(ifelse(best_predictions$Predicted_Class == "Serious", 1, 0), levels = c(0, 1)),
  factor(best_predictions$Actual_Numeric, levels = c(0, 1)),
  positive = "1"
)

data.frame(
  Metric = c(
    "Accuracy",
    "Sensitivity (Recall)",
    "Specificity",
    "Precision (PPV)",
    "Negative Predictive Value",
    "F1 Score",
    "Balanced Accuracy",
    "AUC"
  ),
  Value = round(c(
    cm_best$overall["Accuracy"],
    cm_best$byClass["Sensitivity"],
    cm_best$byClass["Specificity"],
    cm_best$byClass["Pos Pred Value"],
    cm_best$byClass["Neg Pred Value"],
    cm_best$byClass["F1"],
    cm_best$byClass["Balanced Accuracy"],
    as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(
      best_predictions$Actual_Numeric,
      best_predictions$Predicted_Probability,
      quiet = TRUE
    )))
  ), 4)
) %>%
  knitr::kable(
    caption = paste("Full Classification Metrics —", best_model_name),
    col.names = c("Metric", "Value")
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(
    bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
    full_width = FALSE
  ) %>%
  kableExtra::row_spec(
    c(2, 8),
    bold = TRUE,
    background = "#FFF3CD"
  )
```
## Which Inputs Matter Most?

Neural networks do not give coefficients like logistic regression. To make the model easier to interpret, the plot below uses permutation importance. Each feature is shuffled one at a time in the test set. If shuffling a feature causes a large drop in AUC, that feature was important for the model.

```{r nn_permutation_importance, fig.height=6, fig.width=9.5}
set.seed(42)
importance_n <- min(12000, nrow(x_test_scaled))
importance_index <- sample(seq_len(nrow(x_test_scaled)), importance_n)

x_importance <- x_test_scaled[importance_index, , drop = FALSE]
y_importance <- y_test[importance_index]

base_prob <- as.numeric(predict(best_model, x_importance, verbose = 0))
base_auc <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(y_importance, base_prob, quiet = TRUE)))

importance_results <- map_dfr(seq_along(feature_cols), function(j) {
  x_perm <- x_importance
  x_perm[, j] <- sample(x_perm[, j])
  perm_prob <- as.numeric(predict(best_model, x_perm, verbose = 0))
  perm_auc <- as.numeric(pROC::auc(pROC::roc(y_importance, perm_prob, quiet = TRUE)))
  data.frame(
    Feature = feature_cols[j],
    AUC_Drop = base_auc - perm_auc
  )
}) %>%
  arrange(desc(AUC_Drop)) %>%
  slice_head(n = 10) %>%
  mutate(
    Feature = recode(
      Feature,
      year = "Report year",
      month = "Report month",
      num_reactions = "Number of reactions",
      num_drugs = "Number of drugs",
      patient_age_years = "Patient age",
      patient_weight_kg = "Patient weight",
      patient_sex_encoded = "Patient sex",
      age_group_encoded = "Age group",
      drug_count_category_encoded = "Drug count group",
      suspect_drug_encoded = "Suspect drug",
      brand_name_encoded = "Brand name",
      drug_indication_encoded = "Drug indication",
      pharm_class_encoded = "Drug class",
      drug_route_encoded = "Drug route"
    )
  )

ggplot(importance_results, aes(x = reorder(Feature, AUC_Drop), y = AUC_Drop)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, fill = "#2C7FB8", alpha = 0.88) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(AUC_Drop, 4)), hjust = -0.15, size = 3.5, color = "grey20") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(
    x = NULL,
    y = "Drop in AUC after shuffling",
    title = paste("Most Important Inputs for", best_model_name),
    subtitle = "Larger drops mean the model relied more on that feature"
  )
```


### Classification with Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)

```{r train test split}
set.seed(1)
data_train <- data %>% sample_frac(.8)
data_test <- anti_join(data, data_train)
data_test_serious <- pull(data_test, serious)
```

Our next model will be using **extreme gradient boosting** (XGBoost), a tree-based method that sequentially builds and compares trees. Unlike random forest, XGBoost can natively handle NA values, using missingness as another source of predictive information. This makes it an excellent candidate for our messy dataset as it does not require us to drop any observations.

We will use AUC as our classifier criterion because (1) it is better able to handle the class imbalance in our dataset between serious and non-serious reactions and (2) since we are predicting whether a reaction is serious, not the severity, so we do not care about the probability of a serious reaction as much as whether one will occur. We also scale the hyper-parameter scale_pos_weight to better deal with the unequal split between serious/non-serious cases. We proceed with cross-validation of the boosted fit to determine the ideal number of rounds without overfitting.

Our first model included country as a predictor, but we found that due to imbalanced training data, the model used country as its single strongest predictor. The proportion of serious reactions was significantly higher for non-US relative to US patients, so we decided to remove country from the model. The data likely contained more serious reactions abroad because it is FDA-managed and primarily contains reports from American providers and patients; a drug reaction would have to be very serious while abroad to justify reporting. We show the fit with country in the Appendix.

We will proceed by **removing country** as a predictor to see whether boosting yields an accurate model without the effects of over-representation from a single country. We first show a naive fit below:

```{r}

set.seed(1)

# set up data subset, without country

data2_train <- data_train %>% dplyr::select(year, month, serious, num_reactions, num_drugs, suspect_drug, brand_name, drug_indication, pharm_class, patient_age_years, patient_sex, patient_weight_kg, drug_route_label)

data2_test <- data_test %>% dplyr::select(year, month, serious, num_reactions, num_drugs, suspect_drug, brand_name, drug_indication, pharm_class, patient_age_years, patient_sex, patient_weight_kg, drug_route_label)

# build DMatrices

options(na.action = "na.pass")

# train
X_train2 <- sparse.model.matrix(serious ~ . - 1, data = data2_train)
labels2 <- as.numeric(data2_train$serious) - 1
nrow(X_train2) == length(labels2)

dtrain2 <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_train2, label = labels2, missing = NA)

# test
X_test2 <- sparse.model.matrix(serious ~ . - 1, data = data2_test)
labels2_test <- as.numeric(data2_test$serious) - 1
nrow(X_test2) == length(labels2_test)

dtest2 <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_test2, label = labels2_test, missing = NA)
```


```{r}

# define hyperparameters
params <- list(
  booster = "gbtree",
  objective = "binary:logistic",
  eval_metric = "auc",
  eta = 0.1,
  max_depth = 6,
  min_child_weight = 1, 
  gamma = 0,            
  subsample = 0.8,
  colsample_bytree = 0.8,
  scale_pos_weight = sum(labels2 == 0) / sum(labels2 == 1)
)

set.seed(1)

# build first (naive) fit without country
xgb.fit2 <- xgb.train(
  params = params,
  data = dtrain2,
  nrounds = 500,
  verbose = 0,
  evals = list(train = dtrain2, test = dtest2)
)

# classifier
pred_prob2 <- predict(xgb.fit2, dtest2)
pred_class2 <- ifelse(pred_prob2 > 0.5, 1, 0)
mean(pred_class2 == (as.numeric(data2_test$serious) - 1)) # 0.767

# roc curve
roc_boost2 <- roc(data2_test$serious, pred_prob2)
plot(roc_boost2, 
     main = "XGBoost ROC Curve without Country" , 
     col = "blue", 
     lwd = 2, 
     print.auc = TRUE) 

pred_class2 <- factor(pred_class2, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("No", "Yes"))
confusionMatrix(pred_class2, data2_test$serious)
```

We report an AUC of 0.851 and a testing accuracy of 0.767. The model has a more balanced sensitivity/specificity, with sensitivity = 0.748 and specificity = 0.733. It has a significantly higher positive prediction value than negative (0.528 vs. 0.900), suggesting that it makes more errors when guessing that a reaction is not serious and few errors when it is. Generally, this means **we can be more sure when the model predicts a serious outcome than a non-serious one**. 

```{r}

# define hyperparameters
params <- list(
  booster = "gbtree",
  objective = "binary:logistic",
  eval_metric = "auc",
  eta = 0.1,
  max_depth = 6,
  min_child_weight = 1, 
  gamma = 0,            
  subsample = 0.8,
  colsample_bytree = 0.8,
  scale_pos_weight = sum(labels2 == 0) / sum(labels2 == 1)
)

set.seed(1)

# cross validation
cv <- xgb.cv(
  params = params,
  data = dtrain2,
  nrounds = 500,
  nfold = 5,
  stratified = TRUE,
  early_stopping_rounds = 20,
  maximize = TRUE
)
```

We proceed with cross-validation to select the optimal number of rounds. Stopping isn't triggered because the training and test AUC continue to increase with each round. We will cut the model off at **500 rounds** since it reached a **training AUC of 0.917** and **testing AUC of 0.910**.

```{r}

set.seed(1)

# final model without country
final_xgb_2 <- xgb.train(
  params = params,
  data = dtrain2,
  nrounds = 500
)

# classifier
pred_prob_final2 <- predict(final_xgb_2, dtest2)
pred_class_final2 <- ifelse(pred_prob_final2 > 0.5, 1, 0)
mean(pred_class_final2 == (as.numeric(data2_test$serious) - 1)) # 0.767

# roc curve
roc_boost_final2 <- roc(data2_test$serious, pred_prob_final2)
plot(roc_boost_final2, 
     main = "Final XGBoost ROC Curve, without Country", 
     col = "blue", 
     lwd = 2, 
     print.auc = TRUE) 

```

```{r, results = TRUE}

# important predictors
importance2_f <- xgb.importance(model = final_xgb_2)
knitr::kable(head(importance2_f, 15),
             caption = "Most Important Features in Boosted Model, without Country") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# plot a representative tree (first one)
# xgb.plot.tree(model = final_xgb_2, trees = 0)

pred_class_final2 <- factor(pred_class_final2, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("No", "Yes"))

# visualize confusion matrix

confusionMatrix(pred_class_final2, data2_test$serious)

#ggplot(as.data.frame(confusionMatrix(pred_class_final2, data_test_subset$serious)$table), aes(Reference, Prediction, fill = Freq)) +
#  geom_tile() +
 # geom_text(aes(label = Freq), color = "white") +
  #theme_minimal() +
#  theme(legend.position = "none") +
 # labs(title = "Confusion Matrix for Boosted Fit 2")
```

Without country as a predictor, the most important predictors for whether a patient will have a serious outcome are **the number of drugs taken by the patient** and **the number of reported reactions to the medication**. We also see that **year of report, patient age, and month** are important - the decisions based on year and month could be due to seasonal trends or changes in the types of drugs being reported year-over-year. Interestingly, suspectDrugOther being important shows that there are rarely reported drugs associated with serious reactions. Many of the significant variables are `unknown' levels of various factors (drug_indication, drug_route_label, patient_sex, and brand_name), an effect of the model over-fitting to noise within the Unknown reports.

We report a sensitivity of 0.742, specificity of 0.775, positive prediction rate of 0.528, and negative prediction rate of 0.899. This suggests that the model has a **better rate of predicting positive and negative results than previous models**, but still has trouble predicting **non-serious reactions**. It is excellent at classifying serious predictions.

Notably, intramuscularly administered drugs also have a higher rate of serious reactions, confirming findings from our previous EDA.

## Discussion

The overall best model for prediction, as determined by AUC, interpretability, and accuracy, is the **logistic LASSO model**.The LASSO model has higher interpretability and is less prone to overfitting than neural network and boosted models, but lacks classification ability, likely due to imbalanced data split. We recommend the **boosted model** for classification since it has the highest AUC and a relatively balanced sensitivity and specificity, meaning it is not over-predicting serious reactions.

### Logistic Models

The logistic regression model had poor performance as a classifier (AUC = 0.647), over-predicting that a reaction would be serious (sensitivity = 1) and completely under-predicting that a reaction is not serious (specificity = 0), likely due to removing high-cardinality factor predictors. The logistic LASSO model showed strong improvement due to a sparser set of predictor parameters (AUC = 0.849) but still showed a poor specificity of 0.00833, with little improvement in the relaxed LASSO. 

### Neural Network Models

The neural network analyses directly followed from EDA, investigating whether severity is related to patient age, polypharmacy, number of reactions, drug identity, drug class, and route.

The baseline model asks whether a simple neural network is enough. The deeper model asks whether more layers improve the ability to capture interactions. The wide and deep model asks whether a larger network can capture broader patterns without overfitting. Together, the three models show whether extra model complexity actually helps or whether the main signal is already captured by the basic features. The best neural network model was the **deep and wide model** which obtained an AUC of 0.881, sensitivity = 0.952, and specificity = 0.494. However, when considering system resources, the deep and wide model had the most trainable parameters and took the longest to run with only modest improvements to classification ability.

### Boosted Models

The boosted models were a robust way to analyze the dataset without excessive cleaning. The implementation required detailed EDA to explore the effects of different predictors on the probability of a serious outcome; for example, we found that the model over-fit to country and had to re-build without country as a predictor. Tree-based models tend to exploit patterns in data that may not be representative of the overall population which should be kept in mind while modeling. Notably, the boosted model did not include drug identity and route as predictors because they required outside data to correctly process which was not obtained until after the model was built. Future work should include these predictors to determine if they increase predictive power or change the importance rankings among predictors. The most important variables in the model were **number of drugs taken by the patient** and **number of reported reactions**, suggesting that providers should be prudent in checking adverse reactions to medications and be conservative with prescriptions in order to minimize adverse reaction rate. 

## Conclusion

The results should also be interpreted carefully. FAERS is a reporting database, not a controlled clinical trial. A model can identify patterns in reported adverse events, but it cannot prove that a drug caused an outcome. The best use of this model is risk screening and pattern recognition, not clinical diagnosis. Future work may re-fit the boosted model on different predictor variables like drug route, which required supplementary data that was not available when the model was first fit.

## Appendices

### EDA

#### Age

The first question we explore is whether patient age is associated with both the *volume* of adverse event reports and their *severity*. These are two different things — a group can generate many reports without necessarily having the worst outcomes.

```{r age-count, fig.height = 5.5}
age_palette <- c(
  "Infant(0-2)"       = "#E8F5E9",
  "Child(3-12)"       = "#A5D6A7",
  "Teen(13-18)"       = "#66BB6A",
  "Adult(19-40)"      = "#FFF176",
  "Middle-Aged(41-65)"= "#FFB74D",
  "Senior(66-80)"     = "#EF9A9A",
  "Elderly(81+)"      = "#E53935",
  "Unknown"           = "#CFD8DC"
)

data %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  summarise(n = n()) %>%
  mutate(label = comma(n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = age_group, y = n, fill = age_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.6, color = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = age_palette) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Number of Reports",
    title    = "Who Is Being Reported On? Report Volume by Age Group",
    subtitle = "Middle-aged adults dominate the dataset by a wide margin"
  )
```

The dataset is dominated by middle-aged adults (41–65), who account for 166,278 reports — more than the next two age groups combined. This reflects both their large share of the population on prescription medications and the higher burden of chronic disease in that age range. Notably, 152,337 reports have unknown patient age, the second-largest "group" in the dataset, which will need careful handling during modelling.

```{r age-rate, fig.height = 5.5}
data %>%
  filter(age_group != "Unknown") %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  summarise(n = n(), serious_rate = mean(serious == "Yes")) %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste0(percent(serious_rate, accuracy = 0.1),
                   "\n(n=", comma(n), ")")
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = age_group, y = serious_rate, fill = age_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.72, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = label), vjust = -0.3, size = 3.3, lineheight = 1.1,
            color = "grey30") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = age_palette) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, limits = c(0, 1.08),
                     expand = c(0, 0)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    axis.text.x   = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Serious Reaction Rate",
    title    = "How Serious Are the Reactions? Rate of Serious Outcomes by Age",
    subtitle = "Reports with unknown age excluded; n shown per bar"
  )
```

Infants (0–2) stand out with the highest serious reaction rate at 90.3%, despite being among the least-reported groups (only 3,101 reports). This almost certainly reflects a **reporting bias** rather than a biological vulnerability unique to infants — adverse events in babies are considered alarming enough to document regardless, while milder events in the same age group go unreported. Children (3–12) show the lowest serious rate at 67.8%, suggesting that when reactions are reported for school-age children, they are more evenly spread across mild and severe. Among adults, rates are fairly stable (72–81%), with a gradual increase into the elderly.

##### How the Nature of Harm Changes with Age

Knowing that the elderly have an 81% serious reaction rate still masks an important question: what *kind* of harm are they experiencing? The grouped bar chart below separates fatal, hospitalization, life-threatening, and disability rates by age group.

```{r age-outcome-grouped, fig.height = 6.5}
data %>%
  filter(age_group %in% age_order_known) %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  summarise(
    Fatal              = mean(is_fatal),
    Hospitalized       = mean(is_hospitalized),
    `Life-Threatening` = mean(is_life_threat),
    Disabling          = mean(is_disabling)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(-age_group, names_to = "Outcome", values_to = "Rate") %>%
  mutate(
    age_group = factor(age_group, levels = age_order_known),
    Outcome   = factor(Outcome,
                       levels = c("Hospitalized", "Fatal",
                                  "Life-Threatening", "Disabling"))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = age_group, y = Rate, fill = Outcome)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.78) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = percent(Rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.78),
    vjust = -0.35, size = 2.8, color = "grey25"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Hospitalized"     = "#42A5F5",
    "Fatal"            = "#4A148C",
    "Life-Threatening" = "#EF5350",
    "Disabling"        = "#FFA726"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.14))) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  ) +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Rate Within Age Group",
    fill     = NULL,
    title    = "The Nature of Harm Shifts Dramatically with Age",
    subtitle = "Fatal and hospitalization rates dominate in older patients; life-threatening reactions peak in infants"
  )
```

This chart tells a different story depending on where you look. For **infants**, life-threatening reactions are the defining characteristic (11.4%), a rate three times higher than any adult age group — these tend to be systemic or allergic reactions with rapid onset. For **elderly patients (81+)**, it is fatality that stands out: 25.2% of reports in this group end in death, compared to just 6.6% in adults aged 19–40. Hospitalization also peaks in the elderly at 52.5%. The consistency of disability rates across all groups (roughly 2–3%) suggests that permanent impairment is more tied to the specific drug involved than to patient age.

#### Weight


##### Weight

```{r weight-violin, fig.height = 5.5}
data %>%
  filter(!is.na(patient_weight_kg),
         patient_weight_kg > 5,
         patient_weight_kg < 250) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = serious, y = patient_weight_kg, fill = serious)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.55, trim = TRUE, adjust = 1.8, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, outlier.shape = NA, fill = "white",
               color = "grey30", show.legend = FALSE) +
  stat_summary(fun = median, geom = "point", size = 3,
               color = "grey20", show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "#4DBBD5", "Yes" = "#E64B35")) +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("No" = "Non-Serious", "Yes" = "Serious")) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 250, 25)) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(10, 200)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  labs(
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Patient Weight (kg)",
    title    = "Weight Is Not a Meaningful Differentiator of Reaction Severity",
    subtitle = paste0("145,953 reports with recorded weight; values >200 kg trimmed for display\n",
                      "Median: Serious = 72.6 kg | Non-Serious = 74.8 kg")
  )
```

The distributions of patient weight for serious and non-serious reactions are almost identical — the median weights are 72.6 kg and 74.8 kg respectively, a difference of 2.2 kg. The violin shapes, IQRs, and upper tails all overlap. This tells us that weight on its own carries essentially no information about whether a reaction will be serious. This is unsurprising: weight is a proxy for body composition, but the mechanisms driving serious drug reactions — pharmacogenomics, polypharmacy, drug class — operate largely independently of body size. Weight may still appear in a LASSO model as a weak confounder, but it is unlikely to be selected as a primary predictor. It is also worth noting that weight is missing for 72% of reports, which further limits its role.

### Neural Network Support

### Training Diagnostics

The training curves show how each model learned over time. A good pattern is when training and validation loss both decrease and then level off. If training loss keeps improving while validation loss gets worse, that would suggest overfitting.

```{r nn_training_diagnostics, fig.height=6.4, fig.width=11}
history_all <- bind_rows(
  history_to_data_frame(history_baseline, "Baseline shallow neural network"),
  history_to_data_frame(history_deep, "Deep dense neural network"),
  history_to_data_frame(history_wide_deep, "Wide and deep neural network")
)

loss_data <- history_all %>%
  select(Model, Epoch, Training_Loss, Validation_Loss) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Training_Loss, Validation_Loss),
    names_to = "Series",
    values_to = "Loss"
  ) %>%
  mutate(Series = recode(Series,
                         Training_Loss = "Training loss",
                         Validation_Loss = "Validation loss"))

accuracy_data <- history_all %>%
  select(Model, Epoch, Training_Accuracy, Validation_Accuracy) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(Training_Accuracy, Validation_Accuracy),
    names_to = "Series",
    values_to = "Accuracy"
  ) %>%
  mutate(Series = recode(Series,
                         Training_Accuracy = "Training accuracy",
                         Validation_Accuracy = "Validation accuracy"))

loss_plot <- ggplot(loss_data, aes(x = Epoch, y = Loss, color = Series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme(legend.title = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    title = "Training and Validation Loss",
    x = "Epoch",
    y = "Binary cross entropy loss"
  )

accuracy_plot <- ggplot(accuracy_data, aes(x = Epoch, y = Accuracy, color = Series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.9) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  theme(legend.title = element_blank()) +
  labs(
    title = "Training and Validation Accuracy",
    x = "Epoch",
    y = "Accuracy"
  )

gridExtra::grid.arrange(loss_plot, accuracy_plot, ncol = 1)
```


### Boosted Model Support

#### With Country 

```{r}

# remove columns related to outcome (except serious)
data_train_subset <- data_train %>% dplyr::select(year, month, serious, num_reactions, num_drugs, suspect_drug, brand_name, drug_indication, pharm_class, drug_count_category, patient_age_years, patient_sex, patient_weight_kg, country)

data_test_subset <- data_test %>% dplyr::select(year, month, serious, num_reactions, num_drugs, suspect_drug, brand_name, drug_indication, pharm_class, drug_count_category, patient_age_years, patient_sex, patient_weight_kg, country)

# build DMatrices

options(na.action = "na.pass")

# train
X_train <- sparse.model.matrix(serious ~ . - 1, data = data_train_subset)
labels <- as.numeric(data_train_subset$serious) - 1
nrow(X_train) == length(labels)

dtrain <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_train, label = labels, missing = NA)

# test
X_test <- sparse.model.matrix(serious ~ . - 1, data = data_test_subset)
labels_test <- as.numeric(data_test_subset$serious) - 1
nrow(X_test) == length(labels_test)

dtest <- xgb.DMatrix(data = X_test, label = labels_test, missing = NA)

```

#### Boosted Fit 1: Including Country

We first build a naive fit and display the ROC Curve below. For this model, we used year, month, number of reported reactions, number of drugs taken by patient, suspected drug, brand name, condition the medication was prescribed for, FDA pharmaceutical class, polypharmacy bin, and patient demographics (age, sex, weight, and country of reaction). We chose to include each of these predictors to better capture nuanced relationships within the data and to potentially find interacting effects. For the naive fit, we report an AUC of 0.927.

```{r boosted fit}

# hyperparameters
# we start by using AUC criterion
# and binary:logistic to predict outcome (serious/not serious)

set.seed(1)

params <- list(
  booster = "gbtree",
  eta = 0.01, # was 0.1
  max_depth = 4, # was 3
  subsample = 0.5,
  gamma = 1,
  lambda = 0, # was 1
  colsample_bytree = 0.8,
  min_child_weight = 5,
  objective = "binary:logistic",
  eval_metric = "auc"
)

# build first (naive) fit
xgb.fit <- xgb.train(
  params = params,
  data = dtrain,
  nrounds = 500,
  verbose = 0,
  evals = list(train = dtrain, test = dtest)
)

# classifier
pred_prob <- predict(xgb.fit, dtest)
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob > 0.5, 1, 0)
mean(pred_class == (as.numeric(data_test_subset$serious) - 1)) # that's pretty good!! - 0.852

# roc curve
roc_boost <- roc(data_test_subset$serious, pred_prob)
plot(roc_boost, 
     main = "ROC Curve for Naive Boosted Model 1 (Including Country)",
     col = "blue",
     lwd = 2,
     print.auc = TRUE)


```

```{r}

set.seed(1)

# define hyperparameters
params <- list(
  booster = "gbtree",
  objective = "binary:logistic",
  eval_metric = "auc",
  eta = 0.1,
  max_depth = 6,
  min_child_weight = 1, 
  gamma = 0,            
  subsample = 0.8,
  colsample_bytree = 0.8,
  scale_pos_weight = sum(labels == 0) / sum(labels == 1)
)

# cross validation
#cv <- xgb.cv(
#  params = params,
#  data = dtrain,
#  nrounds = 1000,
#  nfold = 5,
#  stratified = TRUE,
#  early_stopping_rounds = 20,
#  maximize = TRUE
#)

```

Using fct_lump can remove rare but deadly reactions, since we are now only considering the 50 most common factor variables. These can be explored with EDA and are left out of the modeling process. It means that the model might not perform well when seeing these rarer drugs, but the model performs very well on more common medications.

Cross validation showed a **high training and testing AUC**, with the training AUC flattening around **0.94** and the testing AUC flattening around **0.929** after **1000 rounds**. Cross validation did not yield a best iteration as the AUC continued to increase, even after 1000 rounds, though the practical improvements bottomed out around **500 rounds**, with training AUC of 0.93 and testing AUC of 0.925. For computational efficiency, we proceed with the 500-round model, reporting the most important features and ROC curve below.

```{r}

set.seed(1)

# final model
final_xgb <- xgb.train(
  params = params,
  data = dtrain,
  nrounds = 500
)

# classifier
pred_prob_final <- predict(final_xgb, dtest)
pred_class_final <- ifelse(pred_prob_final > 0.5, 1, 0)
mean(pred_class_final == (as.numeric(data_test_subset$serious) - 1)) # 0.825

# roc curve
roc_boost_final <- roc(data_test_subset$serious, pred_prob_final)
plot(roc_boost_final, 
     main = "ROC Curve for Final Boosted Model 1 (Including Country)", 
     col = "blue", 
     lwd = 2, 
     print.auc = TRUE) 

# confusion matrix
pred_class_final <- factor(pred_class_final, levels = c(0,1), labels = c("No", "Yes"))

# PLOT heatmap of confusion matrix
ggplot(as.data.frame(confusionMatrix(pred_class_final, data_test_subset$serious)$table), aes(Reference, Prediction, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile() +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), color = "white") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  labs(title = "Confusion Matrix for Boosted Fit 1")



```

```{r, results = TRUE}
# important predictors
importance <- xgb.importance(model = final_xgb)
knitr::kable(head(importance, 15),
             caption = "Most Important Features in Boosted Model") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# plot a representative tree (first one)
# xgb.plot.tree(model = final_xgb, trees = 0)

# convert tree to table
# shows us which features the model splits on first
first_tree <- xgb.model.dt.tree(model = final_xgb)

```

The single split on whether the report came from the United States accounts for nearly **43\%** of the total information the model uses to make its decisions, which is vastly larger than all other predictor variables. This might be because this is a FDA managed data set, which is an American authority and is likely biased towards drug interactions that occurred in the US.

```{r}
plot_data <- data %>%
  count(country) %>%
  mutate(
    pct = n / sum(n) * 100,
    country = reorder(country, -n)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  slice(1:20) %>% 
  mutate(country = reorder(country, -n))

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = country, y = n, fill = country)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = rainbow(nrow(plot_data))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Global Distribution of Reports by Country",
    subtitle = paste0("Total Sample Size: ", comma(sum(plot_data$n)), " reports"),
    x = "Country (Top 20 + Other)",
    y = "Number of Reports",
    caption = "Data from the FDA Adverse Drug Effects Database"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1, size = 9),
    legend.position = "none", 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "#7f8c8d")
  )
```

```{r, results = TRUE}
# rate of serious reaction by country
data %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    serious_rate = mean(as.numeric(as.factor(serious)) - 1)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(total)) %>%
  slice(1:5) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = 'Rate of Serious Reaction, by Country',
               col.names = c("Country", "Count", "Rate of Serious Reaction")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)
```

Not only does the US account for the majority of the entries in the database, but the other most common countries have a **much higher** rate of serious reactions. This may be because only serious reactions to drugs are reported while abroad, whereas any drug interaction is reported domestically.

```{r, results = T}
# table for other metrics

knitr::kable(confusionMatrix(pred_class_final, data_test_subset$serious)$byClass,
             caption = 'Success Metrics for Boosted Fit 1',
             col.names = c('Metric', 'Value')) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# features model splits on
knitr::kable(first_tree[1:7, .(ID, Feature, Split, Yes, No, Gain)],
             caption = "Features the Final Boosted Model (with country) Splits on", 
             col.names = c("ID", "Feature", "Split", "Yes", "No", "Gain")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)
```

#### Without Country (Naive Model)

```{r, results = T}
# important predictors
importance2 <- xgb.importance(model = xgb.fit2)
knitr::kable(head(importance2, 15),
             caption = "Most Important Features in Boosted Model, without Country") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# plot a representative tree (first one)
 xgb.plot.tree(model = xgb.fit2, trees = 0)
```

#### Without Country (Validated Model)

```{r, results = T}
first_tree2 <- xgb.model.dt.tree(model = final_xgb_2)
knitr::kable(first_tree2[1:7, .(ID, Feature, Split, Yes, No, Gain)], 
             caption = 'Ordered Features that Boosted Model (no country) Splits on') %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# confusionMatrix(pred_class_final2, data2_test$serious)

# success metrics from confusion matrix
knitr::kable(confusionMatrix(pred_class_final2, data2_test$serious)$byClass,
             caption = 'Success Metrics for Boosted Fit 2',
             col.names = c('Metric', 'Value')) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)
```


```{r, results = T}
# number of reactions
data %>%
  group_by(num_reactions) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    serious_rate = mean(as.numeric(as.factor(serious)) - 1)
  ) %>%
  slice_tail(n = 10) %>%
  arrange(desc(total)) %>%
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Number of Reactions Reported", "Count", "Rate of Serious Reaction"),
               caption = "Rate of Serious Reaction Outcomes by Number of Reported Reactions")

# number of drugs taken
data %>%
  group_by(num_drugs) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    serious_rate = mean(as.numeric(as.factor(serious)) - 1)
  ) %>%
  slice_tail(n = 10) %>%
  arrange(desc(total)) %>%
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Number of Drugs Taken", "Count", "Rate of Serious Reaction"),
               caption = "Rate of Serious Reaction Outcomes by Number of Drugs Taken by Patient") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)
```

#### EDA For Boosted Model


```{r}
data %>%
  count(num_reactions) %>%
  mutate(
    pct = n / sum(n) * 100,
    num_reactions = reorder(num_reactions, -n)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  slice(1:20) %>% 
  mutate(num_reactions = reorder(num_reactions, -n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = num_reactions, y = n, fill = num_reactions)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = rainbow(20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Reports by Number of Reactions Reported",
    subtitle = paste0("Total Sample Size: ", comma(sum(plot_data$n)), " reports"),
    x = "Number of Reactions (Top 20)" ,
    y = "Number of Reports",
    caption = "Data from the FDA Adverse Drug Effects Database"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1, size = 9),
    legend.position = "none", 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "#7f8c8d")
  )

```

```{r}
data %>%
  count(num_drugs) %>%
  mutate(
    pct = n / sum(n) * 100,
    num_drugs = reorder(num_drugs, -n)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(n)) %>%
  slice(1:20) %>% 
  mutate(num_drugs = reorder(num_drugs, -n)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = num_drugs, y = n, fill = num_drugs)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", color = "white", linewidth = 0.1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = rainbow(20)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Reports by Number of Drugs Taken by Patient",
    subtitle = paste0("Total Sample Size: ", comma(sum(plot_data$n)), " reports"),
    x = "Number of Drugs Taken (Top 20)" ,
    y = "Number of Reports",
    caption = "Data from the FDA Adverse Drug Effects Database"
  ) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, vjust = 1, hjust = 1, size = 9),
    legend.position = "none", 
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12, color = "#7f8c8d")
  )

```



```{r, results = T}
# number of reactions
data %>%
  group_by(num_reactions) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    serious_rate = mean(as.numeric(as.factor(serious)) - 1)
  ) %>%
  slice(1:15) %>%
  arrange(desc(total)) %>%
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Number of Reactions Reported", "Count", "Rate of Serious Reaction"),
               caption = "Rate of Serious Reaction Outcomes by Number of Reported Reactions") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

# number of drugs taken
data %>%
  group_by(num_drugs) %>%
  summarise(
    total = n(),
    serious_rate = mean(as.numeric(as.factor(serious)) - 1)
  ) %>%
  slice(1:15) %>%
  arrange(desc(total)) %>%
  knitr::kable(col.names = c("Number of Drugs Taken", "Count", "Rate of Serious Reaction"),
               caption = "Rate of Serious Reaction Outcomes by Number of Drugs Taken by Patient") %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = "condensed", full_width = FALSE)

```

## AI Usage Statement

- **Debugging and Code Correction:** Claude and ChatGPT was used to identify and fix errors in the R Markdown file throughout the project. This included resolving variable scoping issues in the XGBoost modeling sections (e.g., params object defined out of order), correcting misassigned variable names in the neural network model loading blocks, and fixing incorrect data frame references in confusion matrix calls.

- **Methods Section Drafting:** Claude and ChatGPT was used to help draft the Methods section of this report, drawing on the modeling code already written by the authors and the project slide deck. The statistical descriptions, mathematical notation, and justifications were reviewed and approved by the authors before inclusion.

- **Scope of AI Use:** Claude and ChatGPT was not used to perform any data analysis, make modeling decisions, interpret results, or generate any of the figures, tables, or code in this report. All analytical choices, including model selection, hyperparameter tuning, predictor inclusion decisions, and conclusions drawn from results, are the original work of Suzanna Semaan, Olivia Yang, and Kruthi Muralidhara Jety.
